title: Database Connection Storm from Application Service Account

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST CSF DE.CM-04 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST CSF DE.CM-04 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.34: Database Connection Storm from Application Service Account.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.7.34' running on db:mysql:audit, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=database sourcetype=db:mysql:audit earliest=-6h
| stats count by user, src
| eventstats median(count) as med by user
| where count > med*10 AND count>1000
description: Statistical spike in DB connections by application service account for compromised app or insider detection.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes database and sourcetypes db:mysql:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Database Connection Storm from Application Service Account. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework tells us what good practice looks like, and we keep evidence we match it.
implementation: (1) Baseline per app; (2) correlate with deploys; (3) block at WAF optional. Indexes required: `index=database`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=db:mysql:audit`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: MySQL audit.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `db:mysql:audit`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Baseline per app; (2) correlate with deploys; (3) block at WAF optional.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=database sourcetype=db:mysql:audit earliest=-6h
| stats count by user, src
| eventstats median(count) as med by user
| where count > med*10 AND count>1000
```

Understanding this SPL

**Database Connection Storm from Application Service Account (DE.CM-04)** — Statistical spike in DB connections by application service account for compromised app or insider detection.

Documented **Data sources**: `db:mysql:audit`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): MySQL audit. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: database; **sourcetype**: db:mysql:audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=database, sourcetype=db:mysql:audit, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user, src** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count > med*10 AND count>1000` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Time chart
knownFalsePositives: Detect coverage drops during sensor outages, agent reinstall, or after major reorgs.
